name: Study 5: Cleansing our Play From Errors

comments.text:
This chess game was sent by a subscriber and the rating of both players were low. 

However, rating is just a number. Both low and high-rated individuals are subject to errors. The important thing is eliminating these mistakes from our thinking.
Black won the exchange.
And White placed their rook on the e-file, which wasn't the most active.
The king unpinned.
The bishop could have retreated...
The rook captured the pawn.
This was forced.
And White also waited, giving a check.
Black got their knight into d4, attacking the bishop once more.
Black got their king out of the 8th rank.
The king went to g2.
The king went to f3.
White traded the rooks.
If this, for instance...
This move wouldn't have helped White.

The bishop always controls f3. 

The king will hide on h2 when possible.
White pushed their weakest pawn, trying to get some trades in.
The bishop went to e2.
Because after the check...
The king went back to g5.
The king would have moved to e5.
Ne2 would have probably arrived unprovoked.

If the knight would fall, a desperado tactic would have been the best move. 

This tactic occurs when you're bound to lose a piece so it's good to play a crazy capture before it's taken.
Black went for a pin.
Check.
Black started setting up a mating net.

Black went for a nice idea. But this is the first error: by trying to move their knight - a strategical improvement- they overlooked that their b-pawn was hanging. 

We must always be aware of our opponent's checks, captures and attacks!
But the point was that ...Nc5 would have been really effective.
And White took on d2 as well.
This was a check, controlling the e4-square.
White attacked the knight.

And Black gave checkmate. 

Both sides missed many chances. Black's play with losing the a-pawn wasn't ideal, but White's blunder of losing the exchange allowed Black an easier game at the end.
The bishop went back to the e2-square.
White recaptures...

And White went f4 at this point, which was the wrong moment to do so. 

Black already had complete control of that square.
Black took the bishop.
And Black gained control of the b-file.
And this position is truly dominant, with the king potentially going to e3 aided by the rook.
And the king came back up to f3.
The entire idea is that the king can invade.
The king went to e3.
Black took.
White took. This was the wrong approach.
Since the a-pawn is still an issue and the knight on c5 controls some key light squares.
Black attacked the knight.
The rook went into the 2nd rank.

Black went for a pawn trade. 

This wasn't ideal because it simplifies the position, and not in a favorable way. Black had to look for complications by increasing the pressure, maybe by getting the king to g3 to pressure g2.
Black gave a check.
The king went to f3.

The rook went into c4, attacking the knight. 

This truly exposes the previous ...a4 move because the pawn's defender is attacked.
And Black's rook went to b4.

And White just moved the king up to f3 again. 

So how did Black win this?
White took, attacking the knight.

But the knight attacked the bishop. 

White should have looked for something else to do. Again, we return to a classic endgame mistake: playing without a plan. 

A one move threat does not constitute a plan in the endgame.

Black just captured.

Of course, not the most precise or top engine move, but not egregious enough to jump out of our seats.

And the bishop went back to g4! 

If it was going to allow itself to be taken on g4, why didn't White just leave it there in the first place?
And the rook avoided the trade.
And Black recaptured.
And Black still gets the exchange but with a pawn as well.

And White thought they were pinning the knight to the rook. 

But this simply misses a tactic: a discovered check.
White reinforced their center.

White pushed the g-pawn.

Even though they were losing, they should have gone for a variation that's a bit tougher for Black to find.
White traded.
The king went to e3.
And Black didn't take the bishop right away, but it's completely fine I suppose.
By far the most natural way to progress in my eyes was ...Rb3+.
White pinned the pawn.
The pawn can get all the way to a2 and restrict the White rook on a1 forever.
Black got the king up. The engine wanted to go ...f4+.
Here White should have moved the king and not traded by their own will.
The bishop moves away.
The king moves back...
The knight jumped into safety, this also has a fork in mind.
Suppose White tried dealing with the back rank...
Black took.
The engine gives this as a substantial improvement, stopping ...f4.
Even if the king goes up to attack the bishop.
White can go behind it, for instance. This is quite drawish.
The bishop recaptured.
Check.
The king went into the corner.
The king moves up.
The bishop went all the way back.
Black played the discovered check.
And this is Zugzwang. A pawn or the king must move.

And the king went down to f2. 

However, White has quite a locked down position.
White entered a self-pin.
The king will be safe.

The king went over to f5. This was to be expected.

The key not to lose here is not allowing White's king progress.
But the pawn wasn't safe anymore.
This variation isn't hard to find when you consider that it's all about king improvement and attacking weaknesses.

The knight went to e2, challenging Black's important d4-knight.

If Ne2 is good for White, then Black shouldn't have provoked it with ...h4.

The reason this move is preferred is because it gains space and completely controls the kingside pawns. 

g2 becomes a backwards pawn.
And Black lost their entire advantage.
White went for the knight.

The problem here is that after ...Ke4, White cannot move their king anywhere useful! 

There were two king moves here.
The a-file was a more active file for the rook, attacking the a-pawn.
This attacks the bishop.
Black gave a check.
Black started trying to activate their knight.
Let's play this one as an example.

And Black began to push. 

Was this safe?
Black got a rook into the 2nd rank.

Once again, White placed their rook on a not so active file.

Ra1 was the best.
White took the pawn.
Black made a waiting move.
Check.
The king went to f1.
White would have been too late thanks to the fact that they wasted time to get to a1.

This was a move to defend. 

Let me begin by saying that there aren't any useful discoveries.
This was possible. The pawn cannot be taken because of a deadly back-rank check.
Black had to go after d3 right away.
And Black attacked the queen.
The point, though, is that the a-pawn wasn't used correctly.
The king went to g1.

And the knight went to c5, putting some pressure on e4 and preparing ...a4. 

Still, everytime we have a passed pawn, we cannot just push it. We have to make sure it's defended and we have to see if the defender cannot be removed.
But White defended the rook, eliminating the pin.
And the knight moves away.
Black recaptured...
And now the key defensive maneuver was to keep the bishop on the diagonal and the king on h2.

Black attacked the bishop. 

...Nxg4 was the best move.
For example, if ...Ke4.
This was winning already.
